agent_outputs.raw: The core problem with the board memo is that it attributes a recovery gain to the collections model without controlling for three simultaneous changes: (1) tax-refund season, which increases borrower liquidity and voluntary payments independent of collector behavior; (2) a dialer vendor switch that improved contactability and changed the composition of the worked queue; (3) managerial queue overrides that departed from model rank. The before-after design with no contemporaneous control makes it impossible to apportion the 16% gain to any single factor. The claim of model-driven efficiency improvement is not supported by the available evidence.

Three confounds make the board memo's attribution claim unreliable. First, seasonality: Feb–Apr is tax-refund season. Collections naturally improve during this window regardless of model quality. Second, the new dialer vendor changed which accounts were reachable — agents now worked a different queue composition with better contact rates. Third, manager overrides for hardship and promised-to-pay accounts introduced non-model treatment selection for a subset of accounts. Compounding all three: there is no same-period control group. The combined effect is that the 16% gain reflects a mix of model contribution, seasonal effect, improved reachability, and manager judgment — indistinguishably. To isolate model contribution, a prospective champion-challenger test with a concurrent control is needed.

The evaluation has a fundamental temporal confound. Comparing Nov–Jan versus Feb–Apr crosses a major collections behavior shift (tax-refund season) while simultaneously introducing two other operational changes (dialer vendor and queue override policy). Any one of these alone would make the before-after comparison inconclusive; together they make causal attribution to the model impossible. The memo should acknowledge that the 16% improvement reflects the combined new system under favorable seasonal conditions, not model-specific contribution. A controlled test is required before claiming model-driven efficiency gains.

synthesis: All three independent assessments agree: the board memo's causal attribution is unsupported. The three primary issues identified across all agents are (1) tax-refund seasonality, (2) dialer-driven contactability change, and (3) absence of a same-period control. Manual queue overrides are noted by agents 1 and 2. The issues are genuine and empirically resolvable — they cannot be resolved by argument alone because the counterfactual (what would recovery have looked like in Feb–Apr with the old queue rules and old dialer) does not exist in the current dataset.
all_issues_raised: dialer contactability change alters queue composition
manual queue overrides break treatment fidelity

empirical_test.condition: Prospective concurrent champion-challenger test: randomly assign delinquent accounts to model-ranked queue versus legacy queue within the same calendar period, with same dialer and documented override policy
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: No material difference in recovery rate or recovered balance between model-ranked and legacy-ranked cohorts within the same calendar window
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Model-ranked cohort delivers materially higher recovery than legacy-ranked cohort in the same period under stable operational conditions
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Model adds some lift but the gain is smaller than the 16% board figure, with remaining gap attributable to seasonal or contactability effects
